title: Crystal Dislocations as Atomic Scale Ratchets
authors: Wu-Rong Jian, Yifan Wang, Wei Cai
abstract: The symmetry of a system's response to external stimuli is a fundamental concept in physics and materials science. At the microscopic scale, breaking this symmetry to achieve a rectified response is exceptionally difficult to engineer and remains rare in nature. Conventional micromechanics models of crystalline solids assume a symmetric response to applied stress, where reversing the load simply inverts the direction of defect velocity without altering its magnitude. In this work, we report an atomic-scale, geometry-rooted mechanism that breaks this symmetry. Molecular dynamics simulations of face-centered cubic nickel reveal that dislocations containing atomic-scale jogs exhibit asymmetric mobility under opposite applied stresses, reversing the loading direction triggers significantly higher drag. This asymmetry arises from an unconventional coupling between an atomic displacement vector and the second-order tensorial eigenstrain of the jog motion mechanism. Because jogs are ubiquitous structures in plastic deformation, this discovery challenges classical descriptions of plastic deformation mechanisms, with direct implications to cyclic creep, and opens new pathways for defect engineering to enhance fatigue resistance.
summary: This work demonstrates that jogged dislocations in FCC crystals act as atomic-scale ratchets, exhibiting asymmetric mobility when subjected to cyclic stress. Using MD simulations, the authors pinpoint the physical origin of this asymmetry to a unique coupling between atomic displacement and the jog's strain field. This finding has major implications for understanding fatigue and plastic deformation mechanisms.
main_problem: The primary goal is to identify and characterize intrinsic, atomic-scale mechanisms that break symmetry to produce a rectified (asymmetric) response to external cyclic stimuli.
main_result: Jogged dislocations in FCC crystals exhibit significant mobility asymmetry under opposite applied stresses, which is attributed to an unconventional coupling between atomic displacement and the jog's eigenstrain.
method: The study employs Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations and Nudged Elastic Band (NEB) calculations to model dislocation motion and calculate energy barriers.
model_or_system: The system modeled is the jogged edge dislocation within face-centered cubic (FCC) nickel. The analysis focuses on the local atomic structure at the jog, which acts as an intrinsic 'lattice ratchet.'
key_observables: Dislocation velocity ($v$) vs. applied stress ($	au$), net directional drift under cyclic loading, and the coupling between the atomic displacement vector ($\mathbf{a}$) and the eigenstrain ($\gamma^*$).
important_parameters: Applied shear stress ($	au$), crystal structure (FCC), and the specific geometry of the jog.
main_assumptions: Conventional micromechanics models assume a symmetric response to stress, which the work challenges. The asymmetry is found to be general for jogged dislocations in FCC crystals.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the asymmetry in velocity vs. stress curves (Fig. 1a, S4-S6), net directional drift under cyclic loading (Fig. 1b, S8), and the atomic configurations detailing the force balance during forward versus backward jog movement (Fig. 2, 3).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the general problem of symmetry breaking in materials, to simulating the asymmetric mobility using MD, and finally deriving the microscopic physical origin of this asymmetry through force analysis (NEB calculations).
why_interesting: While focused on solid-state mechanics, the concept of an intrinsic, geometry-rooted ratchet mechanism driven by non-linear coupling ($\mathbf{a}$ and $\gamma^*$) provides an analogy for engineered quantum ratchets or directed transport in mesoscopic quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Velocity of jogged (solid line) and straight (dashed line) dislocation with character angle θ = 30◦as a function of applied stress τ. The inset shows a schematics of the simulation cell containing a jogged dislocation with Burgers vector b and line direction ξ. The dislocation moves on its glide plane with normal vector n. The arrows on the top and bottom surfaces indicate the direction of applied stress τ when it is positive, which causes the dislocation to move in the positive x-direction (forward). (b) Displacement- time curve of the dislocation motion under sinusoidal cyclic loading at a stress amplitude of 30 MPa. (c) Atomic structure of the jogged dislocation under...

Figure 2: (a) Energy minimized atomic structures near Jog 2 of the two states (L and R) before and after jog motion, viewing from the top of the slip plane x-z. ±x′ is the jog motion direction, aligned with the Burgers vector b direction. The jog core is marked as a yellow petagon and the highlighted atom with the cross mark is the core atom that moves in the opposite direction of the jog motion. (b) Side view (x′-y plane) of the atomic structure of Jog 2. The dislocation line is illustrated as a black arrowed line. The coupling between the core atom and the neighboring layers are represented as three colored struts. (c) Simplified schematic of the two states, where State L corresponds to...
Figure 3: Energy barriers of forward and backward mechanisms at different applied stresses. The corresponding inset schematics demonstrate the force analysis of the for- ward and backward mechanisms.
